scenario: A company's brand requires Forms to use specific colours, fonts, button styles -- matching corporate identity. The team wants to customise the visual styling of Tasklist forms beyond defaults.
question: How can Camunda Forms be visually customised / themed?

options.text: Limited theming in Tasklist itself (some configurations may allow brand colours / logo); for deep customisation, embed the form-js library in a custom UI / portal and apply custom CSS / themes. The form schema (BPMN-deployable Form definition) is rendering-agnostic; the renderer applies styling.

Camunda Forms have fixed default styling with no customisation path -- teams must accept the built-in visual design -- this description does not reflect the actual behaviour. The form-js library underlying Camunda Forms is open-source and designed for embedding in custom applications. By embedding form-js in a custom React, Angular, or vanilla JavaScript application, teams can apply custom CSS stylesheets, override component styles, implement custom themes, and even replace individual form components with custom implementations that match their corporate design system.

Apply custom visual styles to Forms by passing CSS class names or style declarations as process variables that the Form renderer applies dynamically -- this is an incorrect mechanism. CSS styling of form-js components is a rendering-time concern handled at the application layer where the form is embedded, not through process variables. Process variables carry business data. While form components can reference variables for visibility conditions or computed values, they do not accept CSS styling instructions through the variable channel.

Tasklist provides complete theming support out of the box, allowing teams to apply any visual design through built-in configuration options -- this overstates Tasklist's theming capabilities. Tasklist may support some branding configuration such as logo replacement or primary colour changes, but deep customisation matching full corporate identity standards typically exceeds what built-in configuration offers. Teams requiring pixel-perfect brand alignment usually need to embed form-js in a custom portal application where they control the entire rendering context.

a.text: Correct. Two paths for Form visual customisation: Tasklist-level theming (limited): Tasklist may support some configuration (logo, primary colour, etc.) per deployment. Verify Tasklist version's capabilities. For SaaS, theming options may be in Console. Embedded form-js (deep customisation): the form-js library (the JS renderer for Camunda Forms) can be embedded into a custom React / Angular / Vue / etc. app. Apply custom CSS, themes, component overrides. The Form schema (deployed BPMN-attached Form) is rendering-agnostic -- embed wherever you need. Practical pattern: Internal Tasklist users: live with Tasklist's default styling (or its theming options); good enough for most internal use. Customer-facing portals: embed form-js in your branded portal; apply your company's design system; users never see Tasklist. This separation (Form schema vs rendering) enables Forms to work in many contexts without duplicating the form definition.

c.text: Incorrect. CSS at renderer / build level, not via process variables.

d.text: Incorrect. Tasklist has some theming; deep customisation typically requires embedding.

Verify Tasklist version's capabilities. For SaaS, theming options may be in Console. Embedded form-js (deep customisation): the form-js library (the JS renderer for Camunda Forms) can be embedded into a custom React / Angular / Vue / etc. app. Apply custom CSS, themes, component overrides. The Form schema (deployed BPMN-attached Form) is rendering-agnostic -- embed wherever you need. Practical pattern: Internal Tasklist users: live with Tasklist's default styling (or its theming options); good enough for most internal use. Customer-facing portals: embed form-js in your branded portal; apply your company's design system; users never see Tasklist. This separation (Form schema vs rendering) enables Forms to work in many contexts without duplicating the form definition.
